content.claim: Hungarian education has documented precedent for administrative/categorical mechanisms creating segregation pathways (e.g., 'Bridge classes' functioning as repositories for Roma pupils), establishing structural risk that SEN/BTMN labeling could similarly interact with school choice to produce segregation
content.context: Fresh live research through the Education finance lens
limitations: Direct evidence linking SEN/BTMN classification to parent-driven school/class mobility or segregation outcomes was not located in search results
Bridge classes example is structurally analogous but distinct from SEN/BTMN pathway; mechanism transfer assumption is untested
No quantification of segregation magnitude or distributional impact by socioeconomic or ethnic characteristics
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
